coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots/indium-doped zinc sulfide heterostructures for enhanced photocatalytic seawater splitting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Yu-Cheng
Chen, Tzu-Jie
Hsu, Po-Chun
coredata.dc:description: This study aims to enhance photocatalytic hydrogen generation through seawater splitting by employing nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-doped CDs) in combination with indium-doped zinc sulfide (In-doped ZnS) heterostructures. N-doped CDs were synthesized via a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, with optimal performance achieved using 1 g of urea at 140 °C. Integrating N-doped CDs with In-doped ZnS enhanced light absorption and overall photocatalytic efficiency. Photocatalytic hydrogen production under visible light irradiation demonstrated that these heterostructures, using a combination of sodium sulfite and sodium sulfide as effective sacrificial agents, achieved significant hydrogen evolution rates (HER). The HER in seawater was nearly 1.83 times higher than in deionized water, attributed to the increased ionic concentrations in seawater, which reduces electron-hole recombination. Comparative analysis with other ZnS-based materials revealed a significant enhancement in HER performance due to indium doping. Moreover, reusability tests showed that the heterostructures retained 92.8 % efficiency in deionized water and 94.2 % in seawater after five cycles, demonstrating excellent stability and durability. The N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures are easy to fabricate and exhibit high efficiency, high reusability, and minimal hazardous reagents. These attributes make them highly promising for future applications in sustainable energy, presenting viable solutions for addressing energy demands and environmental challenges.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic Hydrogen Production
Seawater
Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Dots
Indium-Doped Zinc Sulfide
Microwave-Assisted Hydrothermal
Reusability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The (a) FETEM image, (b) HRTEM image, (c) average particle size distribution, and (d) XRD pattern of N-doped CDs (1 g urea and 180 °C).
The FESEM images of (a) ZnS microspheres and (b) In-doped ZnS nanoparticles. (c) XRD pattern of In-doped ZnS nanoparticles.
The (a) FESEM images, (b) XRD patterns, and (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures grown at different weights of urea.
The (a) FESEM images, (b) XRD patterns, and (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures grown at different reaction temperatures.
The (a, b) FETEM images, (c) SAED pattern, (d) HRTEM image, and (e) EDS mapping images of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures (1 g urea and 140 °C).
XPS spectra of the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures (1 g urea and 140 °C): (a) survey spectrum, (b) N 1s, (c) Zn 2p, (d) S 2p, and (e) In 3d, respectively.

(a) The average HER of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures grown at different weights of urea. (b) The average HER of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures (1 g urea) grown at different reaction temperatures.
The average HER of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures (1 g urea and 140 °C) under the different (a) sacrificial agents, (b) Na2S concentrations, and (c) ratios of Na2SO3 and Na2S.
(a) The average HER of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures (1 g urea and 140 °C) for the DI and different concentrations of SW. (b) The average HER of different photocatalysts for the SW.

(a) PL and (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of the In-doped ZnS nanoparticles and N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures (1 g urea and 140 °C). Kubelka–Munk function vs. the energy of incident light plots of (c) In-doped ZnS nanoparticles and (d) N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures (1 g urea and 140 °C).
The schematic diagram illustrates the photocatalytic mechanism of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures.
Reusability tests of the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures (1 g urea and 140 °C) for (a) DI and (b) SW.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots/indium-doped zinc sulfide heterostructures for enhanced photocatalytic seawater splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Feng Chia University, Taichung 40724, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Feng Chia University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Feng Chia University, Taichung, 40724, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pritzker School of Molecular Engineering, University of Chicago, Chicago, IL 60637, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pritzker School of Molecular Engineering, University of Chicago

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pritzker School of Molecular Engineering, University of Chicago, Chicago, IL 60637, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aims to enhance photocatalytic hydrogen generation through seawater splitting by employing nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-doped CDs) in combination with indium-doped zinc sulfide (In-doped ZnS) heterostructures. N-doped CDs were synthesized via a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, with optimal performance achieved using 1 g of urea at 140 °C. Integrating N-doped CDs with In-doped ZnS enhanced light absorption and overall photocatalytic efficiency. Photocatalytic hydrogen production under visible light irradiation demonstrated that these heterostructures, using a combination of sodium sulfite and sodium sulfide as effective sacrificial agents, achieved significant hydrogen evolution rates (HER). The HER in seawater was nearly 1.83 times higher than in deionized water, attributed to the increased ionic concentrations in seawater, which reduces electron-hole recombination. Comparative analysis with other ZnS-based materials revealed a significant enhancement in HER performance due to indium doping. Moreover, reusability tests showed that the heterostructures retained 92.8 % efficiency in deionized water and 94.2 % in seawater after five cycles, demonstrating excellent stability and durability. The N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures are easy to fabricate and exhibit high efficiency, high reusability, and minimal hazardous reagents. These attributes make them highly promising for future applications in sustainable energy, presenting viable solutions for addressing energy demands and environmental challenges.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have garnered considerable interest as photocatalysts because of their exceptional light absorption capabilities in the visible spectrum and their efficient electron transfer processes [1–4]. These properties enable their extensive use in photocatalysis. Research has shown that integrating CDs with semiconductor nanostructures can significantly enhance the efficiency of photocatalytic processes, including organic pollutant degradation, water splitting for hydrogen production, and CO2 reduction [4–6]. The synthesis techniques for CDs are typically categorized into two main approaches: top-down and bottom-up [1,7,8]. Top-down approaches encompass techniques such as arc discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical methods, and strong acid oxidation, which involve fragmentation bulk materials into CDs by applying high-energy or aggressive acids. Bottom-up methods involve hydrothermal processes, microwave-assisted hydrothermal, ultrasonic, and dehydration or direct heating methods, which carbonize small organic molecules to form quantum-sized CDs [9–11].

The bottom-up approach has achieved the highest luminous efficiency of CDs reported in the current literature [12]. Recent advances in doping CDs have improved their optical, catalytic, and conductive properties and stability, enabling tunable surface properties and multifunctional capabilities [13,14]. The most commonly used doping elements for CDs include nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), boron (B), and phosphorus (P), which can significantly modify their properties and enhance fluorescence quantum efficiency [15,16]. Incorporating N atoms significantly enhances fluorescence quantum yields, with even low levels of N-doping, contributing to forming a new type of electron-trapping surface state that promotes the separation of electrons and holes [17–20].

Zinc sulfide (ZnS) is an important II-VI semiconductor used as a photocatalyst, notable for its broad bandgap ranging from 2.6 to 4.6 eV. It exhibits high electron mobility, remarkable thermal stability, non-toxic properties, and affordability [21,22]. Additionally, ZnS has been extensively studied due to its rich nanostructures and superior physical and photocatalytic properties [21,23–25]. ZnS nanostructures demonstrate strong photocatalytic activity, making them suitable for applications such as the photoreduction of halogenated benzene derivatives, carbon dioxide reduction, organic pollutant degradation, and hydrogen generation through water splitting [21,26]. Due to ZnS's limited visible light activity, doping it with indium (In) is an effective strategy to modulate its bandgap, enhance visible light absorption, and improve electron-hole pair separation, thereby boosting its photocatalytic performance [27–29]. Combining N-doped CDs with In-doped ZnS for photocatalytic water splitting can enhance hydrogen production efficiency by providing additional active sites, improving charge separation, and extending light absorption into the visible light.

The study explores N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures for hydrogen production via water splitting, focusing on synthesis conditions, sacrificial agents, and seawater. The heterostructures synthesized through microwave-assisted hydrothermal processing showed enhanced light absorption and efficient electron-hole pair separation, leading to improved photocatalytic activity. Under optimal synthesis conditions (1 g of urea, 140 °C) and a sodium sulfite to sodium sulfide ratio of 3:1, the materials exhibited a notable increase in hydrogen production. Notably, N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructure conducted in seawater yielded 1.83 times higher hydrogen output than deionized water, underscoring their potential for sustainable hydrogen generation.

The following chemicals were procured from Thermo Fisher Scientific and used without further purification: citric acid anhydrous (C6H8O7, 99 %), urea ((NH2)2CO, 99 %), zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2 H2O, 98 %), thioacetamide (TAA, CH3CSNH2, 98 %), indium(III) chloride anhydrous (InCl3, 98 %), sodium sulfite anhydrous (Na2SO3, 98 %), and sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S·9H2O, 98 %). Deionized water with a resistivity exceeding 18 MΩ cm−1 was used in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize N-doped CDs, 3 g of citric acid and varying amounts of urea (0, 1, 2, and 3 g) were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water and treated with ultrasonic vibration for 20 min. This solution was then placed in a 100 mL Teflon-lined reaction vessel and subjected to microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment at various temperatures for 30 min. The N-doped CDs synthesized with 0, 1, 2, and 3 g of urea are denoted as C:N = 3:0, 3:1, 3:2, and 3:3, respectively. The yield of N-doped CDs (C:N = 3:1) is approximately 0.16 %. For preparing N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures, 0.176 g of Zn(OAc)2, 0.06 g of TAA, and 0.022 g of InCl3 were dissolved into the previously prepared N-doped CD solution (40 mL) through ultrasonic agitation for 20 min. The resulting mixture was subsequently heated to 160 °C for 30 min using microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment. Finally, the products were rinsed several times with deionized water, collected through centrifugation, and dried at 70 °C for 2 h. The N-doped CDs (C:N = 3:1) account for an average of approximately 7.4 % of the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures.

The morphology and microstructure of the samples were examined through field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-4800) and field-emission transmission electron microscopy (FE-TEM, JEOL-2100F). Crystallinity was determined using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D2 Phaser), while X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ULVAC PHI Versa Probe 4) provided insights into surface composition and chemical states. UV–visible absorption spectra were measured with a Hitachi U-2900 spectrophotometer and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded using a Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution equipped with a 325 nm He-Cd laser. Based on the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method, the specific surface areas were analyzed with a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 analyzer.

Hydrogen production experiments were performed at room temperature and atmospheric pressure in a 50 mL sealed quartz reactor, utilizing a Perfect Light PCX-50B multichannel photochemical system. For each trial, 20 mg of photocatalyst was suspended in 50 mL of an aqueous solution containing 0.1 M of a sacrificial agent. A 5 W LED emitting blue light (λmax = 420 nm) was used as the visible light source to drive the water-splitting process. Blue LED light was chosen for photocatalytic experiments because it is significantly more efficient than white LED light at the same power [30]. Hydrogen output was measured using gas chromatography (GC, Shimadzu GC-2014) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD).

Fig. 1a presents the FETEM image of N-doped CDs produced by microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis at 180 °C for 30 min, employing a precursor solution with a citric acid to urea ratio of 3:1. The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1b) illustrates a distinct lattice spacing of 0.216 nm, corresponding closely to the (100) diffraction plane of graphite carbon [31]. Furthermore, statistical analysis of the TEM images indicates that the average size of N-doped CDs is 3.75 ± 0.58 nm (Fig. 1c), highlighting their structural and size uniformity. This uniformity ensures reliable performance in photocatalytic and other potential applications. Fig. 1d presents N-doped CDs' X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern, showing a broad diffraction peak at around 25.5°, corresponding to disordered carbon atoms and amorphous graphite [31].

Fig. 2a reveals that pure ZnS nanomaterials form microspherical structures through the aggregation of nanoparticles, with sizes ranging from approximately 100 to 300 nm. However, after doping the ZnS nanomaterials with indium (In) ions, their morphology changes from microspheres to nanoparticles, as shown in Fig. 2b. Further analysis of the overall crystal structure of In-doped ZnS nanoparticles using XRD is presented in Fig. 2c. The diffraction peaks at 27.4°, 28.7°, 31.5°, 47.8°, 51.8°, 56.7°, and 77.1° were corresponding to the (012), (104), (107), (0111), (118), (205), and (125) crystal planes of the hexagonal phase of ZnS (PDF No. 01-072-9261).

This study examined the influence of different urea amounts on the synthesis of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures by using a constant weight of citric acid as the carbon precursor for the CDs and varying the urea weight as the nitrogen source. Following N-doped CDs synthesis, they were subjected to a reaction with In-doped ZnS precursors through microwave-assisted hydrothermal to form N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures. Fig. 3a presents FESEM images of heterostructures synthesized with 0, 1, 2, and 3 g of urea. As the weights of urea increase, the morphology transitions from flake-like and granular stacking to predominantly microspherical shapes formed by granular stacking. The XRD pattern (Fig. 3b) was conducted to investigate the influence of varying weights of urea on the crystal structure of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures. The findings reveal that the number of diffraction peaks associated with ZnS declines with increasing urea concentration. The peaks become broader, suggesting that excessive urea may negatively impact the crystallinity of In-doped ZnS nanostructures during synthesis. Furthermore, UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 3c) were analyzed for N-doped CDs synthesized with different urea amounts to assess their impact on N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures. The findings indicate that N-doped CDs synthesized with 1 g of urea exhibit superior absorption properties when incorporated into N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures. This improved absorption potential is anticipated to enhance photocatalytic performance by enabling more effective light capture.

This research aims to analyze how varying reaction temperatures during the synthesis of N-doped CDs affect the surface morphology, crystal structure, and optical absorption properties of the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures, as depicted in Fig. 4. The morphology of N-doped CDs was synthesized at various temperatures and further composited with In-doped ZnS nanostructures, as shown in Fig. 4a. The FESEM images reveal a stacked sheet-like and granular morphology. The N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures synthesized at 140 °C and 160 °C exhibit smaller, more uniform sheet and granular structures. Fig. 4b presents the XRD patterns of the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures synthesized at different reaction temperatures of N-doped CDs. The XRD results show that the variation in the reaction temperature of N-doped CDs does not significantly affect the crystal structure. This finding indicates that the formation of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures is a critical step following the synthesis of N-doped CDs, with the high-temperature reactions involved in the synthesis of In-doped ZnS nanostructures playing a key role in determining the crystal structure. Thus, the reaction temperature of N-doped CDs has minimal impact on the crystal structure. In addition, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the N-doped CDs synthesized at different reaction temperatures for the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures were analyzed, as shown in Fig. 4c. The results indicate that the N-doped CDs synthesized at a reaction temperature of 140 °C exhibit optimal absorption properties when incorporated into the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures. This enhanced absorption characteristic is expected to facilitate increased light absorption, which is beneficial for improving photocatalytic performance.

Fig. 5a and b illustrate low- and high-magnification FETEM images of the N-doped CDs/ In-doped ZnS heterostructures. The N-doped CDs were synthesized using 1 g of urea at a reaction temperature of 140 °C. The FETEM images obtained exhibit a morphology characterized by layered, sheet-like structures and granular formations, aligning with the FESEM results shown in Fig. 4a. Further examination through the SAED pattern (Fig. 5c) confirms the polycrystalline nature of the material, displaying a hexagonal wurtzite crystal structure typical of ZnS (PDF No. 01-072-9261). HRTEM analysis reveals lattice spacings of 0.328 nm and 0.193 nm, corresponding to the (012) and (110) planes of pure ZnS, respectively, thereby validating the crystal structure of ZnS, as demonstrated in Fig. 5d. Moreover, energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping images (Fig. 5e) reveal a uniform distribution of zinc (Zn), sulfur (S), and indium (In) elements throughout the heterostructure, along with a trace amount of nitrogen (N) resulting from the N-doping of the CDs. These findings strongly validate the successful synthesis of the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructure, emphasizing its structural integrity and uniformity in composition.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to analyze the elemental composition and oxidation states on the surface of the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures. The full-range XPS spectrum ( Fig. 6a) reveals distinct peaks associated with C, N, Zn, S, and In for the heterostructures synthesized using 1 g of urea at 140 °C. These results align with the findings from the TEM-EDS images (Fig. 5e), which further validated the existence of these elements. Importantly, the carbon detected in the spectrum could stem from various sources, such as the N-doped CDs, leftover organic materials on the sample's surface, or pump oil utilized in the XPS vacuum system [32]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 6b) shows three distinct peaks at binding energies of 398.1 eV, 399.5 eV, and 400.8 eV, corresponding to sp2-hybridized CN–C, porphyrin-like C–N–C, and amino nitrogen in configurations such as N–(C)3 or H–N–(C)2, respectively [31,33]. These features prove successful N doping within the CDs structure. The high-resolution Zn 2p spectrum (Fig. 6c) displays at 1021.7 eV and 1044.8 eV, corresponding to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively. These values confirm the presence of Zn2+ ions within the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures [34]. The high-resolution S 1s spectrum (Fig. 6d) of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures can be deconvolved into two binding energies of 161.6 eV and 162.8 eV, corresponding to those of S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively [35]. Fig. 6e presents the high-resolution XPS spectrum for the In 3d region, showing two distinct peaks at 444.8 eV and 452.3 eV. These peaks are associated with the In 3d5/2 and In 3d3/2 states, respectively, confirming the presence of In³⁺ ions in the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures [36]. The presence of these peaks is crucial, as it directly confirms the incorporation of indium into the heterostructures, a key factor in enhancing their photocatalytic performance. The detection of In³⁺ ions indicates that indium is essential for altering the electronic structure and enhancing charge separation efficiency in the composite materials [37].

Experiments were conducted to evaluate hydrogen production through photocatalytic water splitting, aiming to determine the photocatalytic efficiency of the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures synthesized under various reaction conditions. The reactions were carried out in a mixed aqueous solution containing 0.1 M sodium sulfide, which acted as a sacrificial agent to facilitate the process. A 5 W blue LED light source illuminated the reaction, simulating visible light conditions. This configuration allowed for a systematic evaluation of the photocatalytic performance of the heterostructures, enabling the investigation of how various synthesis conditions affect their hydrogen generation efficiency. The pH value was maintained without adjustment during the photocatalytic water-splitting process to streamline hydrogen production. Fig. 7a presents the hydrogen production efficiency of the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures synthesized with varying amounts of urea. The results indicate an initial increase followed by a decrease in efficiency with increasing urea concentration. Notably, the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures synthesized with 1 g of urea exhibited the highest light absorption (Fig. 3c). The improved visible light absorption properties likely enhance their photocatalytic performance for hydrogen production. Furthermore, the photocatalytic efficiency of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures synthesized at different reaction temperatures was investigated, as illustrated in Fig. 7b. The findings reveal a consistent pattern in which the photocatalytic efficiency initially rises and then declines as the reaction temperature increases. The N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures prepared at 140 °C exhibited the highest light absorption capacity among the various samples. This enhanced light absorption is directly linked to their superior photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency, suggesting that 140 °C is the optimal synthesis temperature for maximizing performance. This trend highlights the critical role of reaction temperature in tuning the optical properties of the heterostructures to achieve improved photocatalytic activity.

This study explores the effects of different sacrificial agents on the hydrogen production efficiency of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures during photocatalytic water splitting, as shown in Fig. 8a. The sacrificial agents tested include 0.1 M concentrations of methanol, formic acid, sodium sulfide, and sodium sulfite. This study aims to identify the optimal conditions for maximizing hydrogen production by examining the influence of each agent on photocatalytic performance. This comparison offers valuable insights into how different electron donors contribute to improving the efficiency of the water-splitting reaction. The results reveal that inorganic sulfides used as sacrificial agents achieved the highest hydrogen production efficiencies. This improved performance can be attributed to the photocorrosion-prone nature of sulfides. Inorganic sulfide ions act as hole scavengers during the photocatalytic reaction, effectively capturing the holes generated [38,39]. This approach inhibits electron-hole pair recombination within the photocatalyst, enhancing charge separation and increasing overall photocatalytic efficiency [40,41]. Additionally, when the material undergoes degradation, sulfur ions from inorganic sulfides can bind with the material, mitigating the photocorrosion of sulfides and thereby enhancing photocatalytic efficiency and stability [42,43]. Fig. 8b shows the effect of different sodium sulfide concentrations on the hydrogen production efficiency of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures. The data reveal an increase in photocatalytic efficiency as sodium sulfide concentration rises from 0.01 M to 0.1 M. However, efficiency decreases when the concentration reaches 0.5 M, likely due to an excess of sulfide anions adsorbed on the catalyst surface, which raises the energy requirement for electron transfer to the surface. Without sufficient energy to facilitate electron transfer, the hydrogen production efficiency during photocatalytic water splitting decreases [44,45].

Hydrogen production through seawater splitting has become a sustainable alternative to traditional methods, aiding in conserving freshwater resources vital for agriculture, industry, and everyday human needs [49,50]. With oceans holding 93 % of the Earth's water, seawater represents a vast resource that can alleviate pressure on freshwater supplies, supporting clean energy generation and addressing global water scarcity challenges [51,52]. Fig. 9a shows the dispersion of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures in 50 mL of deionized water (DI) or different concentrations of seawater (SW), with the addition of 0.1 M mixed sacrificial agents (Na2SO3:Na2S = 3:1) under blue LED light excitation. The average hydrogen evolution rates (HER) for the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures were 298.0 (DI), 362.2 (25 % SW), 429.7 (50 % SW), 519.1 (75 % SW), and 545.3 µmolh−1g−1 (SW). As the concentration of SW increases, the matrix becomes more complex, leading to a gradual enhancement in the efficiency of photocatalytic hydrogen production. These results show that hydrogen production efficiency in SW is nearly 1.83 times higher than that in DI. The improved efficiency is likely attributed to the high ion concentrations in seawater, where specific ions may interact with photogenerated holes, decreasing the electron-hole recombination rate in the photocatalysts and enhancing photocatalytic efficiency [53,54].

To further compare the photocatalytic hydrogen production performance of various materials in seawater, this study examines the effect of optimal reaction conditions on ZnS microspheres, In-doped ZnS nanoparticles, N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures, and N-doped CDs/ZnS heterostructures, as illustrated in Fig. 9b. The average HER for ZnS microspheres, In-doped ZnS nanoparticles, N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures, and N-doped CDs/ZnS heterostructures in seawater were 1.1, 155.9, 545.3, and 0 µmolh−1g−1, respectively. Table S1 presents a comparison of the photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency of N-doped CD/In-doped ZnS heterostructures with other photocatalysts, emphasizing the benefits of their straightforward synthesis process and efficient hydrogen generation directly from seawater. This promising feature underscores their suitability for practical applications, especially in advancing energy efficiency and lowering carbon emissions through sustainable hydrogen production. The specific surface area measurements, listed from highest to lowest, are as follows:191.3 m²/g for the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures, 85.7 m²/g for the In-doped ZnS nanoparticles, and 71.1 m²/g for the ZnS microspheres. These results indicate that the combination of N-doped CDs and In-doped ZnS results in a considerably larger accessible surface area, which enhances the number of active sites and boosts photocatalytic efficiency. In addition, the average HER of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures exhibited nearly 495.7 and 3.5 times higher than the ZnS microspheres and In-doped ZnS nanoparticles. Furthermore, it was found that merely combining N-doped CDs with ZnS did not lead to any measurable hydrogen production, highlighting the absence of photocatalytic activity. This finding highlights the significance of indium ion doping in ZnS, which is crucial in improving the efficiency of photocatalytic seawater splitting for hydrogen production.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy utilized a 325 nm excitation wavelength to assess the electron-hole recombination rates in In-doped ZnS nanoparticles and N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures. The detection range extended from 350 to 800 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 10a. The emission intensity of the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures was significantly lower than that of the In-doped ZnS nanoparticles. This reduction in emission intensity suggests the formation of continuous energy bands between the N-doped CDs and the In-doped ZnS, which promotes more efficient electron-hole pair separation. The lower recombination rate of electron-hole pairs in the heterostructures indicates that integrating N-doped CDs with In-doped ZnS effectively enhances charge carrier separation, improving photocatalytic performance.

Furthermore, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was performed to investigate the absorption characteristics of the materials within a wavelength range of 300–800 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 10b. The N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures exhibited a significant improvement in light absorption from the ultraviolet to the visible spectrum in comparison to the In-doped ZnS nanoparticles. This increased absorption indicates that the N-doped CDs enhance the light-harvesting ability of In-doped ZnS, thereby improving the efficiency of photocatalytic hydrogen production from seawater splitting. The results demonstrate that introducing N-CDs enhances the optical properties of In-doped ZnS and contributes to a more efficient photocatalytic process, making these heterostructures a promising candidate for hydrogen generation applications [55].

The In-doped ZnS nanoparticles' band gaps (Eg) and the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures were assessed using the Kubelka-Munk method, as illustrated in Fig. 10c and d. The calculation was performed using the equation αhν = A(Eg − hν)ⁿ, where A is a constant, α is the absorption coefficient, ν represents the photon frequency, Eg is the band gap energy, and h denotes Planck's constant [56–58]. The parameter n indicates the characteristics of the semiconductor: it equals 1 for indirect bandgap semiconductors and 1/2 for direct bandgap semiconductors. Based on prior research, ZnS is categorized as a direct bandgap semiconductor. Thus, n was assigned a value of 1/2. The calculated band gap for the In-doped ZnS nanoparticles was approximately 2.89 eV. Notably, the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures also exhibited a band gap of 2.89 eV, suggesting that incorporating N-doped CDs did not modify the band gap of the In-doped ZnS. This finding implies that while the heterostructure enhances photocatalytic activity through improved charge separation, it does not significantly influence the intrinsic electronic properties of In-doped ZnS concerning its band gap.

This research delves deeper into the reaction mechanism governing hydrogen production through photocatalytic water splitting utilizing N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures, as depicted in Fig. 11. The band gap of pure ZnS microsphere (Fig. 2a) was calculated at approximately 3.38 eV (Fig. S1a). The band positions of ZnS photocatalysts were determined using Mott-Schottky analysis, which confirmed their n-type semiconductor nature with a flat band potential of approximately −0.98 V versus SCE (Fig. S1b) [59,60]. Considering the typical offset of 0.2 V for n-type semiconductors, the conduction band (CB) potential was estimated to be −0.94 V versus the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) [61,62]. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis (Fig. S2) confirmed the formation of sulfide vacancies in In-doped ZnS nanoparticles, as indicated by a slight shift in g value and a significant change in signal intensity compared to ZnS microspheres [63]. When subjected to blue LED light, the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructure becomes excited, generating electron-hole pairs. Under blue LED light irradiation, electrons within the ZnS are promoted from the valence band to defect states created by indium doping and subsequently elevated to the conduction band, leaving behind holes in the valence band of ZnS. Following this excitation, the photoinduced electrons in ZnS traverse the conduction band and are transferred to the N-doped CDs, which serve as electron acceptors. These N-doped CDs effectively capture the excited electrons, significantly diminishing electron-hole pairs' recombination rate. At the same time, the holes present in the valence band of ZnS participate in oxidation reactions, resulting in the production of H+ ions and molecular oxygen. The H+ ions then participate in reduction reactions with the electrons accumulated on the N-doped CDs, ultimately generating hydrogen gas. Additionally, the N-doped CDs improve the light absorption properties of the heterostructure, further increasing the photocatalytic efficiency of water splitting for hydrogen generation. This elucidation of the mechanism highlights the effectiveness of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures in significantly improving the overall efficiency of photocatalytic hydrogen generation. The interplay between enhanced charge separation, effective electron capture, and superior light absorption establishes a robust framework for advancing photocatalytic technologies for sustainable hydrogen production.

This study examined the stability of N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures by comprehensively assessing their photocatalytic hydrogen production performance across five consecutive cycles. The experiments were performed under uniform conditions, utilizing a 5 W blue LED light as the excitation source, while maintaining a constant pH for both DI and SW, as shown in Fig. 12a and b. The findings revealed a modest yet observable decrease in photocatalytic efficiency across the five cycles. This decline is mainly attributed to the loss of photocatalyst during successive centrifugation, likely leading to a decrease in the active mass of the catalyst. Despite this decrease, the heterostructures retained 92.8 % of their initial efficiency in DI and 94.2 % in SW after five cycles, demonstrating robust stability and effective reusability. This retention of performance underscores the durability of the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures even after repeated usage, suggesting their potential for long-term application. These findings affirm that the N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures exhibit excellent photocatalytic activity and significant resilience and reusability, making them promising candidates for future applications in both research and industrial settings. Their ability to maintain high efficiency across multiple cycles suggests they could be developed further for practical use in sustainable hydrogen production and other relevant photocatalytic processes.

This study presents the synthesis and characterization of N-doped CDs integrated with In-doped ZnS nanoparticles to create heterostructures, which were assessed for their capability in photocatalytic hydrogen production, especially from seawater. Incorporating N-doped CDs into In-doped ZnS significantly improved light absorption, electron-hole separation, and overall photocatalytic efficiency. This enhancement was demonstrated through comprehensive structural and optical analyses, including TEM, XRD, UV–vis spectroscopy, and photoluminescence measurements. Among various synthesis parameters, it was determined that using 1 g of urea for N-doped CDs synthesis and a reaction temperature of 140 °C led to heterostructures with the most optimal light absorption and enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production. Notably, the study explored using mixed sacrificial agents (sodium sulfide and sodium sulfite) to further boost hydrogen production efficiency. The results confirmed that this combination effectively reduced electron-hole recombination and mitigated photocorrosion, improving stability and efficiency. The N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures exhibited superior hydrogen production rates when using seawater, producing nearly 1.83 times more hydrogen than deionized water. This suggests that the natural ionic characteristics of seawater may positively influence photocatalytic activity. The findings suggest that N-doped CDs/In-doped ZnS heterostructures hold considerable promise for sustainable hydrogen production through photocatalytic seawater splitting, presenting a viable approach for clean energy generation while conserving freshwater resources.

Tzu-Jie Chen: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Po-Chun Hsu: Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources. Yu-Cheng Chang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Yu-Cheng Chang reports financial support was provided by National Science and Technology Council. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their sincere gratitude for the financial support provided by Taiwan's National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) under Grant Nos. NSTC 112-2221-E-035-017-MY3 and NSTC 113-2221-E-035-023-MY3.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sun H., Xiao Z., Zhao Z., Zhai S., An Q. Honeycomb-like porous carbon loaded with CdS/ZnS heterojunction with enhanced photocatalytic performance towards tetracycline degradation and H2 generation. Appl Surf Sci. 2023;611:155631.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sulfur vacancies on MoS2 enhanced the activation of peroxymonosulfate through the co-existence of radical and non-radical pathways to degrade organic pollutants in wastewater
